## SECTION 8 — This Day in History (World)
On February 21, 1965, Malcolm X, the massive, incredibly charismatic, and highly controversial African American Muslim minister and human rights activist, was brutally assassinated. He was shot multiple times while preparing to address the massive Organization of Afro-American Unity at the Audubon Ballroom in Manhattan. His massive, uncompromising advocacy for Black empowerment and his subsequent, massive intellectual evolution following his pilgrimage to Mecca made him a massive, towering icon of the global civil rights struggle.

On February 21, 1972, US President Richard Nixon arrived in Beijing, officially commencing a massive, highly secretive, and monumentally historic state visit to the People's Republic of China. This massive diplomatic maneuver, fiercely brokered by Henry Kissinger, completely shocked the world. By aggressively normalizing relations with the massive communist nation, Nixon successfully executed a massive geopolitical wedge between China and the Soviet Union, fundamentally altering the massive balance of power during the Cold War.

## SECTION 9 — This Day in History (India)
On February 21, 1946, the massive, highly militarily significant Royal Indian Navy (RIN) mutiny aggressively expanded into a massive, nationwide general strike. In massive solidarity with the striking sailors protesting British racism and poor conditions, hundreds of thousands of massive industrial workers in Bombay (Mumbai) and Karachi aggressively took to the streets, clashing violently with British military forces. The massive, unified, cross-communal uprising was a massive, critical factor in forcing the British to rapidly accelerate their exit from India.

On February 21, 1896, Suryakant Tripathi, universally known by his massive pen name "Nirala," was born. He became a massive, highly pioneering figure in modern Hindi literature and a massive, foundational pillar of the *Chhayavaad* (neo-romantic) movement. His massive, incredibly powerful poetry heavily defied traditional, rigid metric structures and aggressively championed the causes of the massive, marginalized working class and the deeply oppressed, securing his legacy as a massive, towering intellectual giant of 20th-century Indian literature.
